data.title: Columbia Disciplined Core Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund performance

  • Columbia Disciplined Core Fund (CRDCEX) Institutional shares returned 8.12% for the quarter ended September 30, 2025.
  • The fund performed in line with its benchmark, the S&P 500 Index, which also returned 8.12% for the same period.
  • The fund's results can be attributed primarily to the performance of its model's stock selection measures.
  • For up-to-date performance information, please check online at columbiathreadneedleus.com .

Market overview

U.S. equity markets finished notably higher in the third quarter of 2025, with several indices reaching record closes during the period. The S&P 500 Index rose 8.12%. Large-cap growth outperformed large-cap value, as the Russell 1000 Growth Index gained 10.51%, compared to a 5.33% return for the Russell 1000 Value Index. In a shift from earlier in the year, small-cap stocks were the top performers. The Russell 2000 Index climbed 12.39%, while the Russell 2000 Value Index gained 12.60%, and the Russell 2000 Growth Index rose 12.19%.

Several factors contributed to the market's upward momentum, chief among them rising expectations for a more dovish U.S. Federal Reserve. The anticipated easing cycle began in September with a 25 basis-point (BP) rate cut, following dovish remarks from Fed Chair Jerome Powell at Jackson Hole in August. (A basis point is 1/100 of one percent.)

On the economic front, concerns about labor market weakness persisted, but the unemployment rate remained low due to decreasing labor supply. Consumer spending was resilient, and corporate earnings were strong, with many companies reporting results that exceeded expectations.

Artificial intelligence (AI) enthusiasm continued to support equity markets, with companies exposed to the theme performing well. This included not only software firms and the “Magnificent Seven” stocks (Alphabet, Amazon, Apple, Meta Platforms, Microsoft, NVIDIA and Tesla (TSLA)), but also semiconductor manufacturers, tech infrastructure providers and power companies. While there were concerns about the scale of capital investment and questions around monetization, overall sentiment around AI remained robust.

Within the S&P 500 Index, the technology, communication services and industrials sectors led gains, fueled by AI momentum, strong earnings and rate-cut optimism. Energy and consumer staples lagged, amid falling oil prices and defensive rotation. Investors favored cyclical sectors, driving the index to its best third-quarter performance since 2020.

Model performance

We divide the metrics for our stock selection model into three broad categories: quality, value and catalyst. We then rank the securities within a sector/industry from “1” (most attractive) to “5” (least attractive), based on the metrics within these categories. During the third quarter of 2025, the catalyst and value themes contributed positively to relative results, but the quality theme detracted. Within the catalyst theme, price momentum scored most positively, outweighing negative scores from earnings revision and cash-flow surprise. For the value theme, seven of the eight factors scored positively during the quarter, with free-cash-flow-to-enterprise-value scoring negatively. Within the quality theme, the gross profit margin factor scored the most negative and weighed on the overall quality theme.

Among the risk factors, volatility and beta were the largest overall contributing factors, while the financial leverage factor provided modest negative effects.

Of the 28 factors across the themes, 22 produced a positive score during the quarter.

Within the portfolio’s buy-sell sector model performance, six out of eleven sectors delivered positive results, with materials, health care, and consumer discretionary serving as the primary drivers of relative outperformance. These gains were slightly offset by negative contributions from consumer staples and financials.

Quarterly portfolio recap

Relative to the benchmark, the portfolio continued to remain essentially neutral weight across sectors. However, the allocation effect slightly detracted from overall performance due to the impact of the strategy’s cash holdings. Security selection was the key driver of returns, more than offsetting the negative allocation effect. Selection was particularly strong in the communication services and energy sectors, while choices within information technology and financials weighed on performance.

Top contributors to relative performance:

  • Arista Networks, a provider of high-performance cloud-networking solutions, experienced double-digit price gains during the third quarter. The strategy maintained an overweight position in the stock during the quarter, which contributed positively as the stock outperformed. Midway through the third quarter, the position was trimmed after Arista delivered a strong second-quarter earnings report, exceeding expectations, and issued robust forward guidance, reaching the strategy’s price target.
  • Citigroup, a Global Systemically Important Bank (G-SIB), offers a wide range of financial products and services to consumers, corporations, governments and institutions worldwide. The strategy's relatively overweight position in the stock contributed positively to performance, as Citigroup outperformed. The strategy reduced its holding in the stock as Citigroup's price steadily climbed during the quarter, driven by analysts' growing consensus around a higher price target.

Sector weights (%): fund vs. benchmark as of September 30, 2025

Source: FactSet

Top detractors to relative performance:

  • Fiserv is a global provider of financial technology and payment solutions. The strategy maintained a relative overweight in the stock during the period, which negatively impacted performance as the stock underperformed. Fiserv's stock price declined during the quarter, reflecting analysts' concerns over stretched valuations following its second-quarter earnings report and forward guidance. The strategy responded by adding to the position during the sell-off.
  • Salesforce is an American cloud-based software company. The strategy maintained a relative overweight in the stock during the period, which negatively impacted performance as the stock underperformed. Early in the quarter, Salesforce experienced downward price pressure following a series of news events, including insider stock sales and board changes. The strategy increased its position during the sell-off.

Outlook

U.S. markets have reached multiple record highs this year, demonstrating remarkable resilience despite widespread investor concerns about valuations and economic risks. This mirrors the familiar disconnect between consumer sentiment and spending behavior that we've observed since 2022, where pessimistic surveys contrast with robust actual spending. Investors now exhibit similar behavior, expressing anxiety about market conditions while continuing to drive prices higher through their actions.

The U.S. economy has weathered trade restrictions and policy implementation challenges reasonably well, but warning signs are accumulating. Labor market momentum is slowing, with job growth decelerating from earlier peaks. Inflation remains persistently above the Fed's 2% target, creating ongoing pressure on household budgets, while lower-income consumers show increasing signs of credit stress. The government shutdown that began at quarter-end adds another layer of uncertainty and could trigger at least a temporary economic slowdown if prolonged.

Several positive developments provide counterbalance to these concerns. AI continues driving productivity gains across diverse industries, with companies increasingly finding ways to monetize these technological advances. Corporate earnings have remained strong, and merger activity has picked up, suggesting business confidence in long-term prospects. The Fed's recent communications indicate a more accommodative stance ahead, with rate cuts likely continuing to support both economic growth and market performance.

A significant challenge facing investors is the deteriorating quality of economic data. Survey response rates have declined sharply in recent years, raising questions about reliability and contributing to larger subsequent revisions. The current government shutdown compounds this problem by potentially disrupting the regular flow of economic statistics that markets depend on for direction.

This environment of substantial uncertainty suggests increased market volatility ahead, creating both opportunities and risks. We favor companies with strong capital discipline, high profitability and quality operations. These characteristics provide defensive advantages during uncertain periods, while positioning firms to capitalize on emerging opportunities in a market that increasingly rewards selectivity and patience over broad exposure.

data.title: U.S. Government Borrows $1.8T In 4 Months
data.description:
Current Trends

The government hit the debt ceiling back in January, which blocked any net new debt from being created from January to June. Once the debt ceiling was lifted, the government wasted no time in catching up for all the months where borrowing was frozen. From July through October, the government borrowed an eye-watering $1.8T.

Note: Non-Marketable consists almost entirely of debt the government owes to itself (e.g., debt owed to Social Security or public retirement)

Figure 1: Month-Over-Month change in Debt

The YTD total through 10 months now exceeds full year borrowing for all but 4 years. More than likely, the borrowing for November and December will bring 2025 borrowing to the third worst year ever and even possibly eclipsing the $2.6T borrowed in 2023, making it the worst year on record aside from the 2020 splurge of $4.5T.

Figure 2: Year-Over-Year change in Debt

The Treasury also wasted no time in reloading the cash balance, rising to a total of $1T.

Figure 3: Treasury Cash Balance

The chart below shows both the maturity of the debt and average interest rate. Bessent tried to undo some of the mismanagement of Yellen, who actively shortened the maturity of the debt. But those efforts have also reversed: see blue line with a small recent peak in June followed by a quick collapse as tons of new short-term debt was issued. This was unavoidable, as there is no way the market could ingest that volume of long-term debt in four months.

Figure 4: Weighted Averages

The true danger facing the government is still in the massive interest currently being paid on the debt. It has risen to over $970B per year! The debt breakdown and true federal interest from the budget has deviated slightly. This is likely from the debt ceiling standoff. It will take a few months to catch up.

Figure 5: Net Interest Expense

Assuming the Fed drops a quarter point in December and waits until March to get back to the “neutral” rate, combined with the rolling maturity of the debt, we can forecast out the cost of the debt going forward. Again, the Treasury left “debt affordability” in the rearview mirror in 2021. The Treasury is now absolutely hemorrhaging cash on debt service costs, set to rise to $1.2T in 2027.

Figure 6: Projected Net Interest Expense

Speaking of debt issuance and rollover, the chart below shows the forecasted debt maturing for 2-10 year maturities. Debt rolling over will be $600B higher in 2026 than it is forecasted to be in 2025.

Note: “Net Change in Debt” is the difference between Debt Issued and Debt Matured. This means when positive, it is part of Debt Issued, and when negative, it represents Debt Matured

Figure 7: Treasury Note Rollover

Yield Curve

The yield curve has gone back to positive sloping between the 2- and 10-year. The Treasury was borrowing short-term the entire time it was inverted, which seems like an odd decision. Bessent is now cleaning up that mess, as noted earlier, but there is not really anything that can be done.

Figure 8: Tracking Yield Curve Inversion

Historical Perspective

The chart and table below show how the debt and interest has changed over time.

Figure 9: Total Debt Outstanding

Figure 10: Debt Details over 20 years

Wrapping Up

Many Fed officials and market pundits have called the current fiscal situation “unsustainable.” This is a gross understatement. The current fiscal situation is an absolute train wreck with no way out. It has been called a ticking time bomb for decades. That bomb has gone off as interest rate expense ballooned higher. It is worse than anyone could have imagined.

The debt ceiling was raised. Borrowing has continued unabated, and at some point, there will be a major debt crisis. This problem is no longer 10-20 years away. It is quickly becoming something that could happen at any point.

Original Post

data.title: Liability Management Exercises Flex Their Strength
data.description:
By Dylan Herrmann, CFA and Jack Parker, CFA

While the overall high yield default rate remains low, liability management exercises eclipsed traditional defaults in early 2024 and now represent the majority of defaults in both high yield and leveraged loans.

Liability management exercises (LMEs) have become more frequent—and arguably increasingly important - in the US high yield, leveraged loan, and collateralized loan obligation (CLO) markets over the past several years.

LMEs have emerged as an alternative for highly leveraged companies to avoid or delay costly courtroom default exercises, to restructure existing debt, or to raise additional liquidity.

For companies backed by private equity, LMEs may be a means to protect or improve equity holder positions at the expense of debtholders. While LMEs may offer benefits to borrowers, some creditors - particularly those left outside of any ad hoc groups, or committees of stakeholders that join for greater leverage - may be subject to increased risks or losses.

LME: Let Me Explain

LMEs encompass a broad range of scenarios and are not a one-size-fits-all approach. These exercises can be preemptive, forced by distress, or somewhere in between. Some common objectives among the many types of LMEs that have arisen in recent years include:

  • Amend and Extend: These types of LMEs seek to modify existing terms, which could include pushing out maturities, amending terms such as coupons, or adding payment-in-kind (PIK) provisions.
  • Opportunistic Exchange: In these situations, old debt is exchanged at or near 100 cents on the dollar for new debt with more bond-friendly terms.
  • Distressed Debt Exchange: In distressed situations, issuers may exchange new debt for existing debt. However, the new debt has an inferior structure, such as a lower value or lower priority claim in the debt stack. These types of LMEs are considered default events by traditional rating agencies and have the highest likelihood of unequal, or non-pro-rata, outcomes across bondholders.
  • Consent Solicitations or Covenant Stripping: In these aggressive actions by issuers, the issuer asks for approval to modify contractual terms of existing debt.

The Rise in LMEs

We have written previously about the current strength of the US high-yield market. The market’s average quality remains near highs not seen in years. In general, healthy leverage and strong interest coverage have kept default rates, including qualifying LMEs, below the historical average. On the other hand, we are observing rising defaults among smaller issuers, with current rates closer to the historical average (Exhibit 1).

However, traditional defaults have been eclipsed by LMEs in both high yield and leveraged loan markets over the last few years (Exhibit 2). One could argue the stage was set for LMEs during the low-rate environment that followed the Global Financial Crisis. Investors traded covenants and lender protections in a frenzied reach for yield.

Then in 2022, rates rose quickly, and the prospect of a higher-for-longer backdrop became a real risk, especially for floating-rate borrowers. At-risk issuers sought unconventional approaches to managing their liabilities while avoiding or delaying traditional defaults and the high court costs, slow speeds, and reduced flexibility associated with them.

Behind the Popularity of LMEs

As mentioned above, LMEs can be an alternative to costly traditional Chapter 11 or Chapter 7 court proceedings, which also tend to be lengthy and rigid. LMEs may offer more flexibility through a broader range of out-of-court solutions, and management teams and sponsors typically maintain more control over the potential outcomes. Lastly, by avoiding public bankruptcy filings, issuing companies may minimize reputational risk.

Recovery rates on LMEs may be similar or even higher compared to traditional defaults. However, the potential advantages for sponsors and issuers may come at the expense of certain creditor groups, pitting some creditors against others in what is referred to as “creditor-on-creditor violence.” As key groups wrestle to obtain the best possible terms, other creditor groups may be excluded from negotiations and fare far worse than would be expected in traditional bankruptcy proceedings.

This uncertainty and growing prevalence of LMEs can lead to increased downside in the stressed portion of the high yield and leveraged loan markets. Creditors who lack the scale and/or relationships to get a seat at the table run a high risk of severe dilution and may want to sell at almost any price.

Issuers may want lower prices of their loans or bonds to “capture discount” - satisfy 100 cents on the dollar of liabilities for something less than 100 cents while avoiding severe dilution of the equity.

Therefore, issuers may withhold information from the market or cast the information in a negative light. While these factors lead to lower prices going into and during an LME, a successful outcome can result in much better trading levels when the LME is concluded.

Can LMEs Be Avoided?

Both issuers and creditors may seek to minimize the occurrence or negative impact of LMEs by following, among others:

  1. LME Blockers: Creditors may include contractual provisions in agreements that bar companies from executing LMEs. These often occur at initial issuance.
  2. Cooperation Agreements: These agreements are legally binding contracts between two or more groups of creditors. No two “coop” agreements are the same, and some still lead to non-pro rata deals from a slim minority, referred to as “offensive coops.” The strongest coops have large-scale support from creditors and push for pro-rata treatment of members.
  3. Enhanced Underwriting: Tighter due diligence may include avoiding privates, especially sponsor-backed deals, or avoiding investing in issues that are owned by creditors who have used aggressive tactics in past deals.
  4. Disqualified Lenders Lists: Controlled by the issuer, these lists prevent current debtholders from selling or assigning debt to certain lenders, like aggressive distressed debt shops or litigious, “loan-to-own” lenders. Issuers seek to maintain a friendly creditor group that does not aggressively seek equity. These types of lender groups are willing to collaborate with issuers on out-of-court debt management but not activities that result in equity dilution, in-court-filings, or post-restructuring litigation.

Legal challenges may be pursued post-LME. However, these are both time-consuming and costly.

Obtaining Favorable Outcomes in LMEs

Best practices to help obtain favorable outcomes in LMEs:

  1. Conduct rigorous fundamental research and thorough covenant review.
  2. Join ad hoc creditor groups, if applicable.
  3. Pursue scale, internally or across firms, to potentially provide a single tranche capital solution. In the event of an LME, larger debt holders typically have more influence.
  4. Agree to commit more capital in the restructuring if it makes sense.
  5. Sign any coop agreement if the terms are agreeable.
  6. Maintain strong relationships with company management, sponsors, legal advisors, and other creditors, especially the steering committee. If possible, be on the steering committee.

The sheer amount of high-yield bonds and leveraged loans outstanding and an uncertain interest rate environment make the continued prevalence of LMEs likely. Fundamental research, risk analysis, strong industry relationships, scale, and experience will be increasingly important to stakeholders seeking to obtain favorable outcomes.

data.title: OPEC Monthly Oil Market Report, November 2025
data.description:
A guest post by D C

The OPEC Monthly Oil Market Report (MOMR) for November 2025 was published recently. The last month reported in most of the OPEC charts that follow is October 2025, and output reported for OPEC nations is crude oil output in thousands of barrels per day (kb/d). In the OPEC charts below, the blue line with markers is monthly output, and the thin red line is the centered twelve-month average (CTMA) output.

OPEC 12 output for September 2025 was revised lower by 13 kb/d, and August 2025 output was revised lower by 3 kb/d compared to last month’s report. OPEC 12 output increased by 33 kb/d, with the largest increases from Saudi Arabia (43 kb/d), Kuwait (37 kb/d), Iraq (34 kb/d) and Nigeria (15 kb/d). Iran (-66 kb/d) and Libya (-30 kb/d) saw decreased crude output. All other OPEC members had small increases or decreases of 7 kb/d or less. Combined output from the other 6 members increased by 2 kb/d in October from the September level.

The chart above shows output from the Big 4 OPEC producers that are subject to output quotas and where most of OPEC spare capacity currently exists (Saudi Arabia, UAE, Iraq, and Kuwait). After the pandemic, Big 4 average output peaked in 2022 at a centered 12-month average (CTMA) of 20849 kb/d. Crude output has been cut by 835 kb/d relative to the 2022 CTMA peak to 20014 kb/d in October 2025. The Big 4 may have about 835 kb/d of spare capacity when World demand calls for an increase in output. Since April 2025, the OPEC Big 4 have increased output from 18346 kb/d to 20014 kb/d, an increase of 1668 kb/d in just 6 months (an average monthly increase of 278 kb/d). If the rate of increase of last month for the Big 4 (121 kb/d) continues in the future, OPEC spare capacity would run out in about 7 months. Perhaps this is part of the reason that future increases after December 2025 have been put on hold, as spare capacity will be down to about 595 kb/d by the end of 2025 (assuming a 240 kb/d increase in output by the Big 4 over the next 2 months).

The chart above shows the most recent 36-month average annual increase of 137 kb/d for the Other 6 OPEC group, which excludes the Big 4 and Iran and Venezuela. Iran and Venezuela have seen their output rise over the past 3 years at an annual rate of 364 kb/d, which I believe will fall to zero in the near future. The chart below shows the long-term trend for the OPEC Other 6, which is roughly flat (a small average annual rate of decrease of 0.75 kb/d) over the past 11 years. I expect the Other 6 will see their output return to this flat trend in the next few years.

The only change in the production/demand balance from last month’s MOMR is a 100 kb/d increase in the forecast for Non-DOC liquids production in 2025. This reduces the increase in the call on OPEC by the same amount because the demand forecast is unchanged from last month’s report.

Refinery crude throughput is a measure of World demand for C+C (though imperfect because some crude is burned directly in power plants in the Middle East). OPEC data shows the previous peak was 81.68 Mb/d in 2018. For the most recent 4 quarters (ending 2025 Q3), the average World refinery throughput was 81.77 Mb/d, a new annual average peak and a significant revision from last month’s report at 81.34 Mb/d for the same 4 quarters.

From the November MOMR:

Preliminary September 2025 data show that OECD commercial inventories rose by 6.0 Mb, m-o-m, to stand at 2,845 Mb. At this level, OECD commercial stocks were 37.7 Mb higher than at the same time last year but 22.4 Mb lower than the latest five-year average and 122.3 Mb below the 2015–2019 average.

OPEC has reduced its estimate of US tight oil output in 2025 and 2026 compared to last month’s report by 10 kb/d for both years. Also, the estimate for 2024 increased by 10 kb/d compared to last month’s report (in line with data from the EIA), so the increase in tight oil output from 2024 to 2025 is 20 kb/d lower than last month’s report, while the decrease in tight oil forecast from 2025 to 2026 is unchanged from last month’s report.

OPEC forecasts US C+C output to increase by 130 kb/d to 13.37 Mb/d and to decrease by 110 kb/d in 2026 to 13.26 Mb/d. The latest STEO from the EIA forecasts 13.59 Mb/d in 2025, 220 kb/d higher than the OPEC forecast. In 2026, the EIA expects US C+C to fall by only 10 kb/d compared to the 2025 annual average. For 2026, the difference between the two forecasts is 320 kb/d. If we assume these two forecasts are equally likely, the average of the two is 13.48 Mb/d in 2025 and 13.42 Mb/d in 2026. My guess is the OPEC forecast will be closer to correct.

Above are two forecasts for output from non-OPEC+ nations from the November MOMR. OPEC forecasts Latin America non-OPEC output to increase by 300 kb/d in 2025 and by 500 kb/d in 2026. By deducting Brazil’s output and assuming the rest of the increase comes from Argentina and Guyana, we get an increase of 70 kb/d in 2025 and 350 kb/d in 2026 for the combined Argentina and Guyana output of liquids.

data.title: ABB's Decade-Plus Of Repositioning Efforts Still Leave Room For Debate
data.description:
I could make the argument that ABB (OTCPK:ABBNY) has been in a state of perpetual restructuring and transformation for at least the last decade, with big M&A investments into motion and electrification in 2011-2012, segment realignments and efficiency drives in the mid-2010s, exits from power transmission, turbocharging, and high-voltage products in the early 2020s, and now the disposal of the robotics business and a clear retrenching around electrification and automation.

This certainly has not been all bad – over the last five years, ABB shares have not only handily outperformed the average industrial (roughly doubling the sector return) but also major rivals like Siemens (OTCPK:SIEGY), Schneider (OTCPK:SBGSY), Emerson (EMR), and Honeywell (HON). Even the ten-year comparison is strong, with only Schneider outperforming and showing that ABB’s recent outperformance isn’t just a product of a lower starting point. Likewise, the shares are up about 25% since my last update, leading the group, though the local shares have done modestly less well (up about 11% and modestly underperforming the sector and Emerson).

It's also certainly not all good, though, as the company’s recent Capital Markets Day laid out a vision for growth and margin improvement that isn’t as impressive as what Siemens, Schneider, and perhaps Eaton (ETN) will likely deliver over a similar period. With a robust valuation, I don’t hate ABB by any means, but I think there will be more pressure on management to deliver stronger results to support (let alone expand) the multiple.

Good News At The CMD, But Not All That Could Be Hoped For

ABB’s Capital Markets Day on November 18 was management’s opportunity to deliver its latest vision for the company, particularly in light of the decision earlier this year to sell the robotics business to Softbank for over $5 billion.

There were definitely some positive takeaways. Management is clearly focused on opportunities in electrification and automation that are likely to deliver above-GDP growth for the decade to come, and likely with less cyclicality than the business has traditionally seen. Moreover, the balance sheet is clean, and management is open to tuck-in strategic M&A to further augment the company’s offerings.

Management also raised targets relative to Street expectations for 2028. The target operational EBITA margin range has been lifted from 16%-19% to 18%-22%, with each divisional target a bit above Street estimates for ’28 (Electrification – 24% vs. 23.7%, Motion 20% vs. 19.8%, Automation 16% vs. 15.2%). That “bit above” is modestly disappointing, though, particularly in the context of recent outlooks from peers/rivals like Siemens that offered a little more upside.

That leads me to arguably the biggest issue – the forecast revenue growth rate. A 5% to 7% organic growth target, with another 1% to 2% from M&A, isn’t so special next to the 6% to 10% growth projected by Schneider and Siemens. It’s certainly plausible that ABB is setting a beatable bar, but I do think this arguably lackluster outlook is why the shares didn’t respond particularly well.

ABB’s New Strategic Vision Is Likewise A Mix Of Good And Bad (Or At Least Questionable…)

Looking at the major strategic moves that ABB has made over the last five years, it has been a mixed bag. While the company is now pursuing a decentralized business model that should work well over time, there’s been vacillation here in the past, and it remains to be seen if the board will stick with this strategy for the long term.

One of the big components of the strategy has been M&A and divestitures. The 2017 acquisition of B&R made some sense at the time, but the integration process was flawed and I would argue that it hasn’t boosted ABB’s presence in discrete automation as well as hoped. Other moves, like the exit from the high voltage business, the sale of the mechanical power transmission business, and the sale of the power conversion business, made sense then and now.

The spin-out of Accelleron (OTCPK:ACLLY) and the sale of the robotics business merit a little more discussion. In retrospect, particularly in light of the strong growth in data center capex, I don’t think spinning off Accelleron made ABB better – Accelleron has stronger margins, stronger ROIC, a strong competitive position, and at least a respectable growth outlook.

With the robotics business, I get why ABB made this move, but I still consider it unfortunate in some respects. At around 2.3x-2.4x sales, ABB got a good price for a business that has had huge cyclicality and a negative overall impact on margins. On the other hand, some of the issues with the robotics business stem from a lack of reinvestment in the business (and/or strategic errors in where they directed reinvestment) – ABB never integrated B&R as well as they could have, and the company was arguably slow to invest in new opportunities like cobots and logistics or supporting capabilities like machine vision and standalone software.

Now the company is reconsolidating around a broad portfolio of assets in electrification, motion, and automation.

The Electrification portfolio is strong, and as I’ve discussed before, I think ABB is well-placed to benefit from the ongoing “electrify everything” trend across most industry end-markets, ongoing reinvestment in power generation and distribution, and ongoing investment into automation and controls. Eaton, Schneider, and Vertiv (VRT) are better-leveraged to data center growth, but I wouldn’t sleep on ABB’s opportunities in areas like oil/gas, mining, metals, and general manufacturing as electrification and micro-grids become more commonplace over the next decade.

Turning to Automation, this is another major long-term trend and one that I believe has been amply discussed by me and other writers. ABB remains well-positioned in process automation and has a respectable presence in discrete automation (helped by its electrification and motion assets), but I do think the company’s position in software remains a drawback. While Siemens, Schneider, Emerson, Honeywell and others have invested in standalone software businesses that have augmented growth and margins, ABB’s embedded software approach has been less impactful in my view.

As far as Motion goes, I don’t have particularly strong feelings. It’s a fundamentally solid business, and the growth of industrial electrification and automation is going to require motion products, and ABB has a strong presence across a wide range of motors, drives, and related services.

The Outlook

The 9% adjusted growth in revenue and orders in Q3’25 was encouraging, particularly as both modestly beat Street expectations. The reacceleration in Electrification (revenue up 13% and orders up 10%), with stable-to-growing demand across the business and strong double-digit growth in data centers, was welcome, and at least consistent with the strength that others like Eaton, Legrand (OTCPK:LGRDY), and Schneider have seen.

The immediate outlook for Automation is more mixed. Weakness in the auto, chemical, metals, mining, and pulp/paper segments persists, and economic data across major markets like the U.S., Europe, and China is still “shaky” with respect to the growth outlook over the next 12 months. Some improvements from machinery customers is modestly encouraging, though that’s coming off a low base after a brutal downcycle. At a minimum, I think ABB is better placed here than Siemens, given their respective end-market mixes.

With 2025 coming in a little better than I’d expected and management’s guidance not deviating too much from my prior expectations, my revisions to 2025 and beyond are modest (<3%). I’m looking for five-year revenue growth of almost 7% on recovering industrial/automation spending and ongoing strength in electrification (particularly data centers). Long term, I think 6% annualized growth is attainable.

I expect ABB to get into the low 20%s for EBITDA margins, and I expect free cash flow margins to improve to the 13%-15% range, though more likely on the 13%-14% end of that range. That should drive around an 8% long-term FCF growth rate, but that’s not enough growth to support an attractive fair value on a discounted cash flow basis.

Looking at what the market has historically paid for various levels of margins, returns (ROIC, ROCE, et al), and growth in the past, I think 16.5x to 17.5x forward EBITDA is reasonable, but even the high end of that only gets me a bit above today’s price.

The Bottom Line

In past years I thought the Street overlooked the self-improvement potential at ABB and the company’s leverage to strong long-term secular growth trends. I can’t make that argument anymore, and I have concerns that ABB’s growth relative to rivals like Schneider could be a headwind for valuation. On the positive side, more than half of the sell-side ratings on ABB are neutral or negative, so it’s not like this name is a darling among analysts anymore.

I don’t think this is a must-sell by any means (though weakness in data center capex would likely lead to a sell-off), but I do think the valuation is more “fair” than compelling now.

data.title: Victory Tax Exempt Intermediate-Term Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Summary

Throughout the third quarter of 2025, fixed income performance was positive given the decline in yields across the Treasury curve and tightening in taxable credit spreads. Inflation rose slightly in Q3 and the labor market showed signs of weakening, with the unemployment rate increasing from 4.1% at the end of Q2 to 4.3% at the end of Q3. However, other parts of the economy surprised to the upside. Strong consumer spending and corporate earnings supported an improved quarter-over-quarter annualized GDP reading of 3.8%. The Federal Reserve reduced rates by 25 basis points in September due to potential downside risks to the labor market and indicated that more easing through 2025 and into 2026 is possible. Municipal yields also declined in Q3, with short-term yields declining more than longer-term yields. Declining muni yields and steady demand despite substantial supply resulted in outperformance in municipal debt for the quarter, with the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index returning 3.00%, beating the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, which returned 2.03%. Muni sectors impacted by potential policy changes experienced wider spreads, offering investors attractive entry points into areas like higher education and healthcare. While yields have come off their mid-quarter highs, they remain elevated, offering tax-conscious investors a very attractive entry point, and not just those in the highest tax brackets. The yield of the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index declined to 3.66% in Q3, down from 3.96% at the end of Q2, offering a taxable-equivalent yield of 6.19% (in the highest tax bracket of 40.8%).

Market Review & Outlook

The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index returned 3.00% for the third quarter of 2025. The fixed income sector saw a broadly positive performance due to falling taxable yields. The third quarter return for the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index was 2.03%; for the Bloomberg U.S. Universal Index, 2.13%; and for the Bloomberg U.S. Treasury Index, 1.51%.

See below for an update on some of the key metrics in the muni market that we continue to monitor.

• AAA muni yields (the benchmark rate of the safest municipal securities) declined across all tenors in Q3. Bloomberg AAA muni yields in the belly of the curve decreased slightly in the third quarter, falling the most on the front end of the curve. Yields are still elevated when compared year over year; as a reminder, an increase in bond yields means that bond prices decrease and vice versa. See below for yield changes from 6/30/25 to 9/30/25 at several key maturities.

• 3-year: 2.65% to 2.25%

• 10-year: 3.21% to 2.92%

• 30-year: 4.52% to 4.30%

• Fund flows for municipal bond mutual funds are positive for the year so far at approximately $31.6 billion year-to-date (according to LSEG Lipper).

• Credit spreads (the difference between riskier bonds and AAA bonds) fluctuated a bit from the end of last quarter. The BBB credit spread increased to 1.06% from 0.97% at the end of Q2 2025, while A and AA credit spreads tightened slightly.

We believe that the creditworthiness of muni borrowers will remain strong in the near term, as many borrowers have maintained improved financial positions post-pandemic, and rainy day funds remain at or near record highs.

We remain committed to our core competency of evaluating, taking, and managing credit risk in the municipal market. We continue to build our portfolios bond-by-bond, relying on our assessment of fundamental credit risk and attempting to capture and distribute incremental yield in an effort to drive higher long- term income to our investors. While there might be some volatility in the muni market in the short term, we remain confident that the right approach is to focus on what matters in the long term.

We believe municipal bonds continue to represent an attractive investment opportunity on a relative basis. At the end of the third quarter, the yield on the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index was 3.66%, which is a taxable-equivalent yield of 6.19% (in the highest tax bracket). After factoring in the benefit of the tax exemption, we believe munis look attractive vs. the (taxable) Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, which yielded 4.37% at quarter-end.

Portfolio Performance & Positioning

The Victory Tax Exempt Intermediate-Term Fund (Fund Shares) (USATX) underperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg Municipal 1-15 Years Blend Index, for the quarter ended September 30, 2025. See the table on the next page for more information on returns.

Contributors

• Interest rate effect, as the Fund is slightly longer duration than the benchmark and yields decreased this quarter.

Detractors

• Security selection broadly, most notably in the Healthcare and Education sectors.

• Security selection in the A and BBB rated bond categories.

data.title: PLTE:CA: Tracking PLTR Without Clear Advantage
data.description:
The Harvest Palantir Enhanced High Income Shares ETF - Class A Units (PLTE:CA) is a Hold. The ETF implements modest leverage up to 25% on an underlying holding in Palantir. Additionally, it uses an option layer (primarily covered call writing) with up to 50% of the portfolio covered depending on the market situation and expectations. Currency hedges are also put in place to reduce the impact of currency fluctuations between the CAD and the USD.

Since the underlying is the prime driver of the total returns (after currency hedges), its performance draws parallels with alternative routes in the US markets - PLTY (an option strategy on PLTR), PLTW (a modest leveraged strategy around PLTR), and PLTR itself.

Performance Comparisons

Total return comparisons (which include the effect of reinvested dividends) with PLTR, PLTW, and PLTY reveal critical insights into what to expect from PLTE:CA. In terms of methodology, it is PLTW and PLTY combined. The amount of leverage used in PLTW is slightly lower at 20% than PLTE:CA's 25%. However, while PLTW's leverage is rigid, PLTE:CA's leverage is an active call capped at around the 25% mark. The portfolio advisor can decide to tweak the leverage based on market conditions and expectations. There are minor nuances in the leverage mechanism too. PLTW aims to capture 120% of the total weekly returns in PLTR and resets every week.

In the option layer, PLTE:CA clearly mentions its active deployment of covered calls, capped at 50%, which ensures a reasonable part of the upside is captured even when the portfolio is leveraged at full tilt. PLTY is also active but does not explicitly mention any cap, which opens up possibilities of a full portfolio coverage capping upside capturing abilities from sharp PLTR rallies.

The offshoot of the minor nuances between PLTW, PLTY, and PLTE:CA boils down to a theoretical expectation of what can be summarized as below

  1. Leverages in PLTW and PLTE:CA, although dynamic in the latter and weekly reset in the former, should lead to similar performance outcomes in both. The exact active calls taken in PLTE:CA could impact the performance differential a bit
  2. Both PLTW and PLTE:CA, should have higher returns than PLTR in bull runs. Vice versa in bear territory for PLTR. The magnitude of outperformance should be in the 10-20% zone depending on the active calls made.
  3. PLTY is expected to have lower returns than PLTW and PLTE:CA in bull scenarios. However, it should provide better drawdown protection due to the option layer. It may even generate income in flat markets for PLTR.
  4. For positive returns in either PLTY, PLTE:CA or PLTW, a Bull thesis in PLTR is a must in the long run. PLTY may still survive without it theoretically.

Actual empirical evidence busts a lot of those expectations and helps understand what to expect from PLTE:CA in reality. First of all, a volatile but clearly bullish run in PLTR (where it has jumped up by ~53%), neither PLTW nor PLTE:CA deliver the 10-20% alpha expected. PLTW still delivers some level of consistent outperformance expected of its leverage, but PLTE:CA does trade at lower levels most of the time because of the option layer potentially capping some upside capture despite leverage. So expect PLTE:CA to closely track PLTR with a minor negative bias in bull markets.

In drawdowns, like that seen in August 2025, when PLTR was down by ~17%, PLTY does serve its intended purpose of an option layer cushioning the fall by a limited but effective ~3 percentage points. PLTW also expectedly magnifies the downside. PLTE:CA, interestingly, also underperforms the underlying PLTW, but to a lesser extent. The conclusion about the balance of leverage and options in PLTE:CA can be summarized as an ETF that closely tracks PLTR and under-delivers in most market conditions by a few percentage points. This is in both bull markets and corrections. There may be a few exceptions, but this is a broader trend seen so far. PLTY has a case for flat to negative markets. PLTW has a case if one has a long-term bull thesis on PLTR. But PLTE:CA seems redundant. I do not see a consistent use case for PLTE:CA versus owning PLTR and systematically generating payouts over time.

Gets Yields and Expenses Right

Despite its performance concerns (or rather redundancies), there are a couple of things I like about PLTE:CA. One, the expense ratio is pegged at a fixed 0.4% (excluding other operating fees that vary depending on actual leverage and transactions made). This is far lower than PLTY and PLTW's 0.99%.

Two, PLTE:CA's yield is far more conservative. For an investment made 6 months ago, the yield on cost basis for PLTE:CA is only ~24%, compared to PLTY's ~80% and PLTW's ~55%. Lower yields with similar total return profiles imply fewer NAV erosion concerns in PLTE:CA.

Even Tactically a Hold

The comparisons with PLTW and PLTY are to provide perspectives on where PLTE:CA stands and how well it delivers on its leveraged and covered option premise. The comparisons show an expected middle ground between PLTW's requirement of a bull thesis and PLTY's defensive positioning. Where PLTE:CA fails to impress is its drawdown management. As discussed, it becomes redundant unless it either outperforms PLTR in bull rallies due to its leverage or provides protection in flat/negative conditions due to options. It does neither consistently.

Even tactically, the need for a bull thesis is not emphatically met at the moment. I don't have a bull thesis for the immediate near term for PLTR. I find valuations stretched now for sustained momentum. I also remain concerned about declining cash flow yields and high stock-based compensation expenses. For a very tactical near term, I am more comfortable deploying something like PLTY. If investors have a long-term bull thesis on PLTR and don't mind the intermediate hiccups, then a leveraged play like PLTW makes more sense, as I have argued here. Unfortunately, PLTE:CA does not fulfill that purpose either, because its option layer seems to nullify the impacts of leverage to a large extent. Overall, looking at the empirical performance so far, I feel PLTE:CA is a Hold, both for tactical and long-term investments. A far simpler and better alternative exists in direct PLTR investments.

data.title: Cameco Corp.: Buy The Dip On Uranium Strength
data.description:
Cameco Corporation (NYSE:CCJ) is one of the world’s leading uranium producers and a key player in the global nuclear energy ecosystem. Headquartered in Saskatoon, Canada, Cameco specializes in the mining, conversion, and sale of uranium on a global scale. Their segments include Uranium, Fuel Services, and Westinghouse, a name that has gained some attention in the nuclear world recently.

With Uranium being one of the primary fuel sources for nuclear power, CCJ is positioned nicely with a portfolio of uranium assets, including world-class mines in Canada and joint ventures in Kazakhstan.

CCJ is up 65% YTD, but down about 20% over the last few weeks. I think the correction provides a great buying opportunity for a leader in the world uranium supply. Q3 results were modest on the financial side, but the long-term outlook for uranium and nuclear power leaves me excited for CCJ. Valuation is raised compared to the market average but is at a steep discount compared to just a few months ago. I rate CCJ a buy.

Q3 Report

CCJ reported EPS of $0.05 and revenue of $435.6 million, both missing expectations significantly. It is YoY growth in earnings but a drop in revenue and not what analysts or investors anticipated, hence the major drop in share price over the last couple of weeks.

EPS revisions have shifted pessimistically, with 12 of 14 Seeking Alpha analysts giving the stock a down revision. In the words of one of the greatest investors of all time, Warren Buffett, I think it’s a great time to be “greedy when others are fearful".

There was a marginal net loss for the quarter, and while the metrics came across as suboptimal, management remained firm on their 2025 outlook, strongly stating they are still on pace to meet year-end forecasts of 20 million pounds of Uranium produced. Sales volume was down, but it was noted that customer requirements and timing of deliveries vary largely, and tracking sales quarterly is not of importance.

Even with the disappointing earnings results, there was some positivity mixed in. The first nine months sales are about 14% higher than a year ago. Cash from operations was nearly triple a year ago in the same quarter. Net income margin is 15%, double the sector median, and up largely from 9.7% last year. Analysts suspect net income margin could rise to over 30% in the next few years. I think these earnings results need to be taken with a grain of salt. While some parts were disappointing, the broader view is still strong, and the deeper outlook may be even better.

Westinghouse

About two years ago, CCJ entered an agreement giving the firm a 49% stake in Westinghouse, with Brookfield Asset Management owning the other 51%. This transaction gave CCJ access to the nuclear reactor technology and services businesses, as Westinghouse is a worldwide leader in this space. On October 28, it was announced that the U.S. government, CCJ, and Brookfield will begin a partnership to deliver $80 billion of new reactors across the United States with Westinghouse technology. The current administration has advocated much harder for nuclear versus other cleaner power sources. This is a huge reason why I remain high on the nuclear field over solar, wind, and other renewables. Cameco CEO says that CCJ, as a reliable Uranium supplier, will support Westinghouse through this development, providing a great long-term global growth opportunity. It is unclear how this will exactly affect CCJ sales down the road, but based on the high volume of new reactors, the amount of fuel projected to be needed will be enormous, and that is exactly where CCJ comes in. This is the biggest wide-lens win for CCJ right now.

Uranium/Nuclear Demand

Demand for Uranium is rising as nuclear projects gain ground, from data centers to industrial projects and more. President Trump is looking to drive America to the “forefront of the nuclear renaissance,” and CCJ has the chance to reap large benefits in the next few years. Total Uranium demand in nuclear reactors is expected to be up 28% through 2030 and to double by 2040. With the Westinghouse partnership accelerating, CCJ will need to gear up for the gap in supply and demand. 31 countries have pledged to triple their nuclear power by 2050. CCJ has 439 nuclear reactors in operation across nearly the entire globe. They are established as well as any company in the uranium industry to meet the growing demand for uranium to fuel the nuclear boom.

Uranium spot price has fallen off since early 2024. In that same time, CCJ's share price has doubled, even with the commodity down over 20%. If CCJ can still increase sales dollars at the same time that the value has dropped, it’s just another promising signal of where the company is heading.

Valuation

This could be confusing, considering CCJ holds a PE ratio of 97x, but I think the stock's valuation is one of the most intriguing pieces. With a forward PE of 84x, right now CCJ is well below its average from the last year or so, even with the strong gains. The last time CCJ had a PE for several months sustained this low was late summer 2024, when the stock has now doubled in value since. I know that this valuation is extremely inflated against the broader market, but the PEG ratio of 0.27, half of the sector average, and the PE at one-third of where it was a year ago leave me seeing this stock actually at a discount.

Risks to Thesis

While CCJ has continued to climb as Uranium spot price has dropped, there still is the possibility that if the commodity keeps falling, there could be a negative impact on CCJ sales. Fortunately, the demand should be around for the foreseeable future, and Uranium should rise from my perspective. Valuation is clearly high compared to the entire market, and there is a case to be made that could be an issue for the stock, but as I explained, I truly think the stock is oddly trading at a discount. Lastly, there is operational risk in this industry, but like a few of their active mines, McArthur River and Cigar Lake, things seem to be smooth sailing, and are still going to be active for years to come.

Conclusion

The recent Q3 report came in lighter than many investors may have hoped, and now the stock has corrected quickly. Financials are still on the rise over the course of the year, when compared to years past. Margins are continuing to grow, and demand for carbon-free nuclear power is only in the early stages. CCJ has global assets and has partnered with an eager U.S. administration to run with the nuclear field. The stock is shockingly trading at a discount in my eyes, and I think the recent price drop is the perfect opportunity to get in on the Uranium segment leader.

data.title: Lithium Argentina: Waiting For A Significant Pullback
data.description:
Investment thesis

I recently sold my Lithium Argentina (LAR) shares, together with my entire lithium mining position in my portfolio, on the significant upswing in its share price in mid-October. Following the significant rally, there was an almost equally significant pullback, which led me to start rebuilding my position in this stock. It has since rebounded, so I am back to having it as a hold.

Lithium Argentina has a strong production growth profile, which may occur at just the right time, as the lithium market is starting to stabilize, likely followed by a robust and sustained rally, perhaps a year down the line. In the meantime, I am waiting on opportunities to buy more, on the expectation that it will pay off in the long term.

Shifting From A Buy To A hold

Since my last coverage of Lithium Argentina, the stock is up about 55%, but significantly down from recent highs.

I sold my position in this stock in October and decided to buy back shares on the recent dip. I intend to add to my position incrementally if the stock goes down below my most recent buying price of just over $4/share, so for now, this stock is a hold.

Lithium Argentina is losing money on low lithium prices

For the third quarter of this year, Lithium Argentina lost $64 million, a sharp increase from a loss of $2.4 million in the same quarter of 2024. This is, in part, a reflection of a company that is in the early stages of ramping up output, but also a reflection of weak lithium prices. Revenues came in at $59 million for the quarter.

As we can see, lithium output is expected to increase for this year; however, it is not likely to translate into profits. Lithium Americas saw a 2% increase in the sale price of its lithium, but the costs per tonne sold increased by 3%. The main takeaway is that it will need significantly higher lithium prices to become sustainably profitable.

A strong production growth profile

The most important aspect of Lithium Argentina's profile as a lithium mining company within the context of an assumed lithium price rally down the line is its potential for output growth in the coming years.

Assuming that the lithium market turnaround will happen and it will be robust, and Lithium Argentina will successfully execute the lithium output growth plan currently in place, we could potentially see an increase in revenues of tenfold or more, depending on how high lithium prices will go.

Gangfeng forecast brings lithium prices to life

My investment decisions are most often made with multi-year fundamentals in mind, which sometimes leaves me somewhat comically exposed to shorter-term headline-driven market moves. Such is the case with my decision taken a month ago to start liquidating my lithium mining positions at a more or less breakeven level. As of right now, a small position in Lithium Argentina and Lithium America (LAC) is the only lithium mining-related exposure I have in my portfolio.

It is within this context of my investment decisions that the Gangfeng forecast of lithium prices potentially doubling or tripling next year, on an expected increase in global lithium demand of 30%, left me caught out, with my long-term considerations seemingly not suited to current market realities. Needless to say, lithium miners are seeing their stock price surge on the headline, with little consideration given to the question of whether or not the forecast has a foundational basis or not, and I managed to get off the lithium ride just in time to miss it.

The roughly 10% jump in lithium prices in a single day, based on this one forecast, suggests that the market believes that it has some merit. Either that, or there is a general self-fulfilling expectation in place that markets will move on headlines; thus, the markets move, only to be brought back to the fundamentals once the effect of the headline on the market fades.

My viewpoint on the lithium market outlook for next year is that it will mostly fail to meet demand growth expectations. The global EV market is currently driven by China and Europe, both of which are experiencing significant economic headwinds.

One of the obvious problems with the current EV market growth trajectory is that its main market, namely China, is quickly reaching the saturation point, with well over half of all new vehicles sold in that market now comprised of EVs. Given China's gradually slowing economy, overall auto sales are not likely to grow.

Europe, the second-largest market for EVs, is faced with an outright stagnated economy. Auto sales are currently down by about a third compared with 2019 levels. The EU has a mandate in place for all personal vehicle sales to be EVs by 2035, but there are growing voices of concern over the potential negative effects it may have on the domestic auto industry. I believe that the short-term as well as the longer-term outlook for European EV sales is very uncertain. It may grow, decline, or stagnate. It is anyone's guess.

Aside from the two main EV markets, the rest of the world is contributing very little to global EV sales. One might argue that we are on the cusp of an EV sales boom in the rest of the world, but there is no obvious catalyst to make that happen. Looking at the world's main EV markets in detail, I find it hard to try to visualize where Guangfeng's forecast of lithium demand rising by 30% next year comes from. It is not impossible, but I do find it improbable.

A sustained oil price rally will trigger lithium demand growth

As I have stated in the recent past, in my analysis of Sociedad Quimica (SQM), a real, sustained lithium price rally is most likely to occur after oil prices see a sustained rally. EVs can become a more enticing substitute when gasoline prices are rising, because the overall cost of buying and owning an EV becomes more attractive relative to ICE-powered cars once the costs of charging an EV versus filling the gasoline tank are factored in.

As far as prospects of a sustained oil price rally, the OPEC+ production increase of about 2 mb/d this year makes it very unlikely for a rally to happen before the second half of next year. I do not see any major contributors to global oil production growth beyond the OPEC+ increase; therefore, we can expect the global oil market to tighten next year. As I pointed out in a recent article, if Russia ends up having to reduce oil exports as a result of sanctions, we may get an oil price spike as soon as next month; however, it would most likely be brief. It would not last long enough to stimulate EV sales. It might have the opposite effect if it leads to a global demand destruction event.

To get the 30% increase in global lithium demand next year, as envisioned by Guangfeng, the rest of the world, aside from China & Europe, will have to contribute significantly to EV sales growth. There are reasons to believe that China & Europe will both disappoint in terms of EV sales next year; therefore, I do not see a path to such growth unless the US, Japan, India, and other markets around the world see a dramatic increase in EV sales. Because most of these markets are lacking governmental incentives and mandates to make it happen, the market has to send the signals to make it happen. I, personally, do not see it happening until 2027.

Conclusions

I sold all my lithium positions in the past month, taking advantage of the recent perking up of lithium mining stock prices. Lithium Argentina's share price saw a significant decline from recent highs of over $5/share to under $4/share this month, which is when I decided to start buying back shares. Its share price is currently $5.5/share, and as such, I have it as a hold, because I believe there is a good chance to buy more shares at under $4/share.

My longer-term outlook for this company is based on Guangfeng's lithium price forecast coming to fruition, not next year as predicted by them, but beyond, where the market price will at least double. At that point, Lithium Argentina will see increased lithium sales from its expanding mines. Even if some ramp-up delays will occur, it could still see a doubling in lithium output within a few years, based on projects currently unfolding or planned.

Assuming revenues will quadruple in the next few years, based on mined production attributable to the company, combined with higher lithium prices, yearly revenues have the potential to reach approximately $1 billion, up from the expected $250 million this year. Assuming a 10% net earnings margin, we could see $100 million/year in earnings. As I write this, the company has a market cap of $880 million. Based on several assumptions, at the current share price of $5.50, the company would have a P/E ratio of approximately 8 to 9.

We should keep in mind that years down the line, this company will still be seen as a growth stock; therefore, we can assume that it will be trading at a higher P/E ratio, perhaps as high as 30, in line with the S&P 500 (SPY) currently. In other words, if assumptions on lithium prices, output growth, and profit margins pan out, Lithium Argentina's share price could be trading multiple times higher than current levels by the end of the decade.

The obvious risk to my strategy on Lithium Argentina is that the Guangfeng forecast of a robust year for lithium price growth next year will turn out to be correct for 2026. In that case, I will have to be content with what is now a minor position in the lithium mining segment of the stock markets. Generally, I prefer to risk missing out rather than risk losing money on an investment.

Looking beyond the current lithium price slump that started in late 2022, which is only now showing early signs of recovery, I do believe that there will be a sustained lithium price rally, perhaps starting in a year or two, with rising oil prices being the trigger. I intend to continue rebuilding my lithium mining positions in expectation of this turnaround. In the meantime, we will most likely have months of disappointing EV sales that will provide us with buying opportunities, which is why, for now, I am content with holding my current modest position in Lithium Argentina stock.

data.title: Nature's Sunshine Looks Fairly Priced After Its Recent Rally
data.description:
My investment thesis for Nature’s Sunshine (NATR) is that its autoship program and focus on the Asian markets are now paying off, so its long-term growth rate has increased. Nature’s Sunshine just posted very strong third-quarter results. With recurring sales from online customers and rapidly growing markets in Asia and Europe, it can continue to post strong results in the future, although its performance might be choppy because of its MLM model. Additionally, this company has a strong balance sheet and will also benefit from stock buybacks. But it appears that the current price of Nature’s Sunshine now reflects all of these strengths.

In the third quarter of 2025, Nature’s Sunshine reported that revenue rose 12.0% to $128.3 million and diluted EPS rose 30.4% to $0.30. The company beat analysts’ estimates on both the top and bottom lines and raised its guidance for 2025. It also said that its adjusted EBITDA rose 42% to $15.2 million. In short, this was a great quarter. After the report, Nature’s Sunshine’s stock soared from around $14 per share to nearly $20 per share in the first half of November.

Nature’s Sunshine also has a new CEO now. Kenneth Romanzi, an experienced CPG executive who was previously the CEO of B&G Foods, became the CEO of the company in October. The company’s previous CEO, Terrence Moorehead, announced that he would step down earlier in 2025. So, the change in management is another reason why Nature’s Sunshine could now grow faster than it has in the past. But the first thing I’ll discuss here is the company’s international growth prospects. Unlike most of the small CPG companies I’ve covered recently, Nature’s Sunshine primarily sells its products to customers outside of the US.

Nature’s Sunshine Has a Global Customer Base

With $128.3 million in total revenue in the third quarter, Nature’s Sunshine reported $64.7 million in revenue from Asia, $22.1 million from Europe, and $5.3 million from Latin America and other markets. North America only contributed $33.6 million, or 26.2%, of the company’s revenue. Additionally, its Asian segment posted 17.1% growth and its Europe segment posted 12.7% growth, while its US segment posted 7.7% growth and its Latin America/other segment posted -13.1% growth. Asia and Europe appear likely to generate even higher shares of this company’s revenue in the future.

So there are several additional factors to consider for this stock. Many domestic food manufacturers are saying the US economy is weak right now and expect poor sales in the short term, but that factor is not as important for Nature’s Sunshine because Asia is its main market. You could think of Nature’s Sunshine as a company that sells supplements to consumers in Japan, China, and South Korea. While Nature’s Sunshine does appear to be benefiting from the MAHA (Make America Healthy Again) movement in the United States, the US is not its main growth market.

Nature’s Sunshine, like large CPG manufacturers, is also exposed to foreign currency fluctuations. In the third quarter, it benefited from the strength of the US dollar and posted additional revenue growth because of that factor, but the situation might change in the future. If it wasn’t benefiting from favorable currency exchange rates, its 3Q 2025 growth would have been 10.5% instead of 12.0%. That’s still double-digit revenue growth, though.

The Asian Supplements Market Is Experiencing Rapid Growth

Because Nature’s Sunshine’s growth primarily depends on the Asian market, I’ll use data for that specific segment to estimate the growth rate of its industry. According to Precedence Research, the Asian supplements market was worth $148.62 billion in 2025 and was expected to grow at a 7.2% CAGR to $273.24 billion in 2034. Additionally, Asia makes up 36% of the global supplements market right now. Many Asian countries now have more elderly consumers, and these consumers are buying supplements to maintain their health.

The Asian supplement market is very competitive, but it’s large and fragmented enough that Nature’s Sunshine currently has a very low market share and has lots of room to expand its share further. Nature’s Sunshine’s main competitors are other MLMs that sell supplements, such as Herbalife (HLF) and Nu Skin (NUS), as well as other supplement manufacturers that sell supplements to grocery stores and health-food stores.

Online Orders and the Benefits of Autoship

While Nature’s Sunshine sells lots of supplements through its global MLM network, its products are also available through its website, and a growing share of its customers are now ordering them that way. So, the company is investing in improvements to its online store. It’s also created an autoship program that gives consumers additional store credit when they set up recurring monthly purchase orders for supplements.

On the 3Q 2025 earnings call, CFO Shane Jones said that over half of the company’s digital customers have now signed up for the autoship program. He described it as a win-win situation. The autoship program provides consistent revenue for Nature’s Sunshine, and it also gives consumers better deals and ensures that supplements are available for consumers to take on a regular schedule. Some supplements don’t work very well if you don’t take them regularly.

Another benefit I’d add is that an autoship program also provides additional convenience to consumers. I discussed this topic in my article about Chewy (CHWY), which has an autoship program that delivers healthy dog food to its customers. With this program, dog owners don’t have to worry about running out of dog food and going to the store to buy more. Chewy automatically drops off dog food on a regular basis. And Nature’s Sunshine is doing the same thing with supplements.

The Main Risk for Nature’s Sunshine Is Price Competition

Nature’s Sunshine’s gross margin is much higher than the gross margins of most health-food manufacturers or health-food stores. So, this MLM is selling high-quality supplements for high prices. Nature’s Sunshine is an upscale health brand, and it’s likely that many of its customers are primarily concerned about ingredient quality rather than getting the lowest prices. But Jeff Bezos once said, “Your margin is my opportunity”. His company, Amazon (AMZN), now owns Whole Foods, which sells high-quality supplements and can compete on quality with Nature’s Sunshine.

Other MLMs also have high margins, so Nature’s Sunshine’s margin isn’t unusual for its industry.

Comparative Valuation for Nature’s Sunshine

Even after its large recent rally, Nature’s Sunshine still has a low valuation. After the market close on November 18, it had a forward EV/sales ratio of 0.59 and a forward EV/EBITDA of 5.89. But other MLMs are trading at low valuations right now as well. On the same date, Herbalife had a forward EV/sales of 0.56 and a forward EV/EBITDA of 4.39. And Nu Skin had a forward EV/sales of 0.35. According to SA, no forward EV/EBITDA was available for Nu Skin, but it had a TTM EV/EBITDA of 3.42.

So, while Nature’s Sunshine has a low valuation in comparison to other CPG companies and stores, it’s trading at a premium compared to other MLMs. MLMs are controversial, so some investors won’t buy their stock. It does appear that Nature’s Sunshine is moving away from the MLM model and investing in its online store right now, but that could make its network members unhappy.

Estimating the Value of Nature’s Sunshine

In the third-quarter earnings release, Nature’s Sunshine guided for $476-$480 million in full-year revenue and $47-$49 million in adjusted EBITDA. After its strong results this quarter beat expectations, I’d say that it’s likely that 4Q results will come in at the top of the range. Its sales for the first three quarters were $356 million. So, if I subtract $356 million from $480 million, I can project that its fourth-quarter revenue will rise 5% to $124 million. And if I divide $480 million in revenue by $49 million in adjusted EBITDA, Nature’s Sunshine will have a 10.8% adjusted EBITDA margin for the full year of 2025.

On the call, CFO Shane Jones explained that Nature’s Sunshine’s sales fluctuate because of the way its MLM business works, but he also sounded confident that its main markets could deliver mid-single-digit revenue growth in 2026. So, I’ll project that Nature’s Sunshine’s revenue will rise 6% to $508.8 million in 2026. With a 10.8% EBITDA margin, its EBITDA would be $55.0 million. Because it’s trading at a premium versus Herbalife and Nu Skin, I’ll project that its EV/EBITDA ratio will drop to 5.5 in 2026, which would result in an EV of $302.5 million.

I can then subtract $14.2 million in debt and add $95.6 million in cash and $6.2 million in other assets to estimate that Nature’s Sunshine will have a $377.6 million market cap in 2026. The shareholder’s equity section of its third-quarter earnings report says that a non-controlling interest owns $6.2 million in equity. Nature’s Sunshine has a strong balance sheet and is buying back its shares, so I’ll project that its diluted share count will drop to 18.3 million in 2026. If I divide a market cap of $377.6 million by 18.3 million shares, I get a target price of $20.63. Nature’s Sunshine’s closing price was $20.82 on November 18, so my target price is 1% lower than the current price.

There’s no current rating on this stock from SA analysts, but Wall Street and the SA Quant rating say it’s a strong buy. Wall Street has a target price of $22, though, which isn’t that much higher than the current price.

Conclusion

Nature’s Sunshine posted very strong third-quarter results in 2025, but it also looks like its results in 2026 are likely to fluctuate from quarter to quarter. The company’s management does expect growth in 2026, but its results this quarter appear to be an outlier. And Nature’s Sunshine just experienced an extremely strong recent rally as well, so while it is gaining autoship customers and its Asian market base is growing, these factors appear to be priced into its stock now. So, I say Nature’s Sunshine is a hold.
